coredata.dc:description: The over usage plastics have possessed serious threat to the ecological system. Thus progressive advancement in fabricating biodegradable and renewable bioplastics is persuasively required to furnish an effective alternative to non-biodegradable plastics. In this view, the current work highlights the production of starch based bioplastic films using waste Colocasia esculenta (taro herb) as a viable starting precursor. The functional ability of developed taro starch based film was further modified by incorporating carbon dots (CQDs) fillers generated from the waste slurry produced during starch extraction from taro herbs. The optimization of films production was achieved by varying the CQDs amount (0.4 %, 0.8 %, 2 % and 4 % w/w) on taro-based films using casting technology. The data illustrates that the addition of CQDs has the ability to enhance the fluorescence property, mechanical properties (Tensile Strength 0.332–4.635 MPa, Elongation at break 42.45–547.63 %) and water resistance ability of films (Moisture content 15–6.4 %, Water Solubility 50–30 % Water Vapour Transmission Rate 2.0012–1.0054 g−2 h−1 and Water Contact Angle 40.6–89.6°). The developed films are found to be thermally stable. The formed films possessed anti-oxidative abilities which safeguard the film from oxidative attacks and ultimately protect the film from the external environment. The fluorescence nanosensor probe has further been developed by utilizing CQDs embedded in a starch-based bioplastic nanocomposite. The developed sensor displayed selective sensing ability towards Fe2+ ion with high sensitivity and accuracy in aqueous medium. Thus, the proposed sensor in this work offers a portable, efficient, low-cost, disposable, non-lethal, and eco-friendly nanosensor for on-site monitoring of metal ion for the food, beverage, and pharmaceutical industries. This is one of the primary reports where metal ions sensing is reported for Taro@CQDs nanocomposites based films. Our outcomes of this work hold significant relevance to providing a smart sensory and biodegradable probe for metal ion sensing by using waste resources, thus offering a better and sustainable alternative for environmental remediation applications.

(a) Fluorescence studies showing the selectivity analysis of TS@CQDs4 for different metal cations (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of TS@CQDs4 with different concentrations of Fe2+ (c) Concentration dependent calibration with linear fit SV plot for TS@CQDs4 in the presence of Fe2+ and (d) Interference studies for the detection of Fe2+ in the presence of other metal ions.

head.ce:title: Bioplastic films from starch of Colocasia esculenta and its waste: A smart template for sensing applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The over usage plastics have possessed serious threat to the ecological system. Thus progressive advancement in fabricating biodegradable and renewable bioplastics is persuasively required to furnish an effective alternative to non-biodegradable plastics. In this view, the current work highlights the production of starch based bioplastic films using waste Colocasia esculenta (taro herb) as a viable starting precursor. The functional ability of developed taro starch based film was further modified by incorporating carbon dots (CQDs) fillers generated from the waste slurry produced during starch extraction from taro herbs. The optimization of films production was achieved by varying the CQDs amount (0.4 %, 0.8 %, 2 % and 4 % w/w) on taro-based films using casting technology. The data illustrates that the addition of CQDs has the ability to enhance the fluorescence property, mechanical properties (Tensile Strength 0.332–4.635 MPa, Elongation at break 42.45–547.63 %) and water resistance ability of films (Moisture content 15–6.4 %, Water Solubility 50–30 % Water Vapour Transmission Rate 2.0012–1.0054 g−2 h−1 and Water Contact Angle 40.6–89.6°). The developed films are found to be thermally stable. The formed films possessed anti-oxidative abilities which safeguard the film from oxidative attacks and ultimately protect the film from the external environment. The fluorescence nanosensor probe has further been developed by utilizing CQDs embedded in a starch-based bioplastic nanocomposite. The developed sensor displayed selective sensing ability towards Fe2+ ion with high sensitivity and accuracy in aqueous medium. Thus, the proposed sensor in this work offers a portable, efficient, low-cost, disposable, non-lethal, and eco-friendly nanosensor for on-site monitoring of metal ion for the food, beverage, and pharmaceutical industries. This is one of the primary reports where metal ions sensing is reported for Taro@CQDs nanocomposites based films. Our outcomes of this work hold significant relevance to providing a smart sensory and biodegradable probe for metal ion sensing by using waste resources, thus offering a better and sustainable alternative for environmental remediation applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The excessive usage of non-biodegradable plastic is one of the primary sources of environmental pollution [1]. However, single-use plastic products, including packaging materials (plastic-based crockery items and pet bottles), are considered one of the major contributors towards plastic pollution [2]. Up to now, the recyclability of generated plastic waste is one of the primary means to tackle the associated pollution rate [3]. However, the recyclability process is found to be highly challenging and consequently leads to generate landfills, thus ruining precious resources and producing a deteriorating influence on the ecological system [4,5]. Thus the fabrication of advanced bioplastics materials is highly recommended approach to contract the imprints of commercially used plastics.

For the challenges associated with plastic pollution, the scientific community has come forward with various alternatives via utilizing biomass feedstock to fabricate biodegradable plastics with advanced properties and high efficiency to substitute synthetic petrochemical-based non-biodegradable plastics [6]. Till date, different biomass sources loaded with starch, pectin, cellulose and chitosan has been employed for the production of bioplastics [7]. However, starch based natural biodegradable plastics displayed admirable film forming aptitudes with better control over the transparency and odour [8]. The low mechanical strength, non-conductive nature and reduced barrier properties of starch based films have restricted their widespread usage [9]. For this reason, various research groups have been trying hard to escalate the working efficiency of biomass derived bioplastics by adding a diverse range of external fillers [10].

In this regard, Colocasia esculenta (taro) is used for the fabrication of bioplastic films in this work. It is one of the monocotyledonous perennial herbs possessing 85 % of starch as total dry mass [11]. The amylopectin component in taro starch supports the formation of hard gels with great elasticity with colorless and odorless films [12]. The existence of A-type crystallinity helps the extracted starch to withstand high temperatures and shear stress conditions [13]. As per the statistical data, it has been found that 30 % of taro is damaged during the process of harvesting, shipping, storage and trading [14]. Thus, the direct dumping of taro waste produces financial damages and wastes the reserves. Therefore, exploring the effective means to convert these taro wastes into superior quality of bioplastics has held significant interest [15]. Various researchers use taro herb as a source of starch for edible film production. However, none of the studies address the issue of residual waste slurry generated as a by product of starch production. The chosen methodology fully utilizes taro fruit. The residual slurry after extracting starch was further used to produce carbon dots (CQDs) without producing any kind of toxic waste in an eco-friendly manner. CQDs, a member of carbon family with a particle size of <10 nm is highly advanced material in environmental remediation applications [16]. They possess enormous unique properties such as low cost, excellent chemical stability, environmental sustainability and good compatibility [17]. The functional groups present on the surface of CQDs provide thermal stability, luminescence, biocompatibity and antioxidant efficacy [18]. Due to their nanoscale dimensions and adaptable surface functional groups, CQDs demonstrate significant interactions and high adsorption capacity with biological surfaces and possessed higher potential in metal ion sensing [19,20]. Metal ion toxicity poses a significant threat due to its adverse impacts on the environment and human health. Researchers from diverse fields, including chemistry, biology, materials science, and environmental science, are currently focused on developing innovative, cost-effective methods for detecting trace levels of desired metal ions [5]. Some of the conventional techniques used for the detection of different analytes were X-ray fluorescence spectrometry liquid chromatography, and inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry [5]. However, the high cost of instrumentation, time-intensive procedures, and limited sensitivity have hindered the widespread adoption of these traditional methods for metal ion sensing. Therefore, the current wok offers the significant potential in the development of advanced bioplastics with metal ion sensing ability to contract the imprints of commercially used plastic materials with high sensing aptitude.

The novelty of the current work is the ability of this methodology to expand to large scale production of sustainable taro starch-based film composites with CQDs as fillers (Fig. 1 ). The developed biocomposite films have displayed significant influence on the thermal, rheological, mechanical and hydrophobic properties of taro starch-based bio-films. The developed films also offer the potential role in on-site detection of metal ions with high selectivity and accuracy. The biocomposite films have displayed superior anti-oxidative properties. In contrast to commercial plastics, this taro@CQDs based bioplastic was found to be entirely degraded in the natural environment conditions, displaying characteristic properties of closed-loop cycle of bioplastic.

Colocasia esculenta (taro) herbs were obtained from a local market of Sector 15, Chandigarh, India. Sodium metabisulphite and 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) were procured from Himedia with 99 % purity. Glycerol was purchased from Sigma Aldrich with 99 % purity. The respective chloride salt of different cations including Li+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Gd3+, Ba2+, Na+, Fe2+, Co2+, Hg2+, K+, Cu2+, and Mg2+ were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich with ≥99 % purity. All the chemicals were used as such without any kind of processing. The respective sample preparations were done using distilled water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction and purification of starch from Colocasia esculenta
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, tubers were peeled off followed by washed with tap water to remove any external impurities [21]. The cleaned tubers were weighed and homogenized in a commercial blender with 0.1 % sodium metabisulphate followed by magnetic stirring for 6 h at 500 rpm. Afterward, the obtained slurry was filtered using double layered muslin cloth. The extracted filtrate was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 15 min to obtain the solid starch, which was washed multiple times with water and ethanol until the pure starch settled at the bottom with transparent supernatant. The remaining slurry was re-suspended in distilled water to extract maximum starch. The obtained powder was dried overnight at 40 °C followed by grinding and sieving through 100 μm mesh.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of CQDs from residual slurry of Colocasia esculenta
ce:section.ce:para: The residue slurry left after starch extraction from Colocasia esculenta was taken as the precursor source for the synthesis of CQDs. Initially, the moisture content of the slurry was removed by placing it in an oven at 70 °C for 3 h. The obtained residual waste (10 g) was further calcined with muffle furnace at 300 °C for 3 h followed by pulverization with pestle and mortar. The obtained powder after calcination was suspended in distilled water for 24 h. Afterwards, the solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min followed by filtration and drying to obtain CQDs powder.

Taro starch based films incorporated with CQDs derived from residual slurry obtained after the extraction of starch from taro were formed by employing a solution casting technique (Fig. 1). Briefly, the respective solution for the films was developed by dissolving 2.5 g of taro starch in 25 ml of distilled water with 1 ml of glycerol as a plasticizer. The resultant mixture was thoroughly mixed under stirring conditions at 250 rpm for 180 min at 50 °C. The bio-composite films with different percentages of CQDs ranging from 0.4 %, 0.8 %, 2 % and 4 % w/w were likewise obtained by mixing with a homogeneous taro starch solution. The gelatinization was performed at 85 °C for 30 min. CQDs-loaded taro starch solution was further casted uniformly using petri plates. The obtained films were further cleaned in a coagulation bath having the mixture of acetone; methanol and ethanol followed by drying and were stored for 48 h in a humidifier at room temperature before any subsequent use. Film obtained using pure taro starch was chosen as control, and named TS. The formed films with CQDs were assigned as Taro starch-CQDs 0.4 % (TS@CQDs1), Taro starch-CQDs 0.8 % (TS@CQDs2), Taro starch-CQDs 2 % (TS@CQDs3), and Taro starch-CQDs 4 % (TS@CQDs4), according to the amount of added CQDs.

The micro-structural investigation of as prepared TS and TS@CQDs films were assessed by field emission-scanning electron micrograph (FE-SEM, JEOL JSM 7500F) at 15 kV. The cross sections of films were pre-treated with liquid nitrogen followed by gold sputtering for 40s and then observed at 15 kV. The morphological characterization of obtained CQDs was carried out using a transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, Hitachi H-7500 electron microscope operated at 100 kV, Japan).
Perkin Elmer (RXI) Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer (FTIR) in the working range of 4000–400 cm −1 was employed to assess the functional groups on the pure TS, CQDs and biocomposite films.

The crystallinity in pure taro starch, CQDs and biocomposite films were acquired using the Panalytical X'Pert Pro X-Ray diffraction (XRD) with monochromatic Cukα radiation (λ = 0.1541 nm and working range 2θ = 5–90°).
Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out using the SDT Q-600 instrument by heating the samples from 25 to 1000 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min under nitrogen-controlled environment.

The mechanical properties of the developed films were checked by calculating tensile strength, Young's Modulus and elongation at the break by employing Biss Universal Testing Machine (UTM) interlinked with a computer operating system. The respective measurements were done using Futek load cell of 400 N, crosshead speed of 1 mm/min. The film size was taken at about 2 × 4 cm and average values were recorded for each sample.

The water contact angle with films was measured using deionised water droplets (5 μl) placed on the surface of biofilms using DSA 10 Mk 2 (Kruess) equipped with an image processing system. The respective images were recorded and contact angle values were calculated using drop shape analysis system (DSA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Moisture content of biocomposite TS@CQDs films
ce:section.ce:para:
The moisture content of the developed films was calculated by measuring the initial weight of the respective sample (chosen: 0.5 g). Afterwards, the respective films were placed in a conventional oven for 24 h at 50 °C [22]. The final weight of the films was further correlated with the moisture content by using the following equation:

The water solubility capacity of the developed films were assessed using Eq. (2) by taking the initial weight of dried films and then placed in glass vials filled with 15 ml water for 24 h. [22]. Afterward, the films again placed in a oven for 24 h at 50 °C and weighed again to get the value of Ws.

Where, Wo is the initial weight of the sample and Ws is the final weight of the sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water vapour transmission rate of biocomposite TS@CQDs films
ce:section.ce:para:
The water vapour transmission rate was calculated by ASTM E96 method [23]. Briefly, the chosen glass bottles were filled with 50 ml of distilled water and covered with the developed films. The edges of the respective bottles were sealed with the Teflon tape followed by paraffin wax. The initial weight of the bottle was measured (Wi). Afterwards, the bottles were placed in a desiccant filled with calcium carbonate and sealed with a silica gel and weighed again after every 1 h, until the constant weight of the film was obtained. The final weight (Wf) of the sample refers to the weight loss after 24 h of exposure.
(3)

W i

W f

x

× 100
Where Wi refers to the initial weight of the bottle, Wf refers to the final weight of the bottle, A is the area of the mouth of the bottle and T is the time interval.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-oxidative properties of biocomposite TS@CQDs films
ce:section.ce:para:
The anti-oxidative properties of biocomposite TS@CQDs films were checked using DPPH radical scavenging method [24]. For the analysis, 1 g of film was mixed in 20 ml of methanol and was placed aside for 24 h. The solution was then centrifuged for 10 min at 4000 rpm. Afterwards, 5 mL of the obtained solution was mixed with 5 ml of methanol solution of DPPH (50 mg/l). The respective solution was kept in the dark for 1 h at room temperature and then absorbance was measured at 517 nm using a UV visible spectrophotometer. The DPPH scavenging rate was estimated by using the following equation.
(4)
DPPH radical scavenging rate
%
=
1 −
A s
A DPPH
× 100
Where ADPPH and As are the respective absorbance value at 517 nm for the methanol and assay solution of DPPH, respectively.

The dried films prepared using different compositions of CQDs were buried 15 cm down the soil for 30 days in a pot [25]. The soil was constantly watered under room temperature conditions. The extent of degradability was assessed after one week interval and changes were captured using a digital camera.

The application of the developed films was further assessed as a metal ion sensing template. For the analysis, the casting solutions of each film were diluted and stirred continuously for 24 h. After stirring, the respective solution was sonicated for 15 min. The fluorescence measurements were performed on a Perkin Elmer LS-55 spectro-fluorometric instrument in the working range of 200–800 nm. The emission intensity of each sample was assessed and found that TS@CQDs4 gives the maximum intensity. Thus, the metal ion sensing experiments were done using TS@CQDs4 as standard. The diluted casting solution of TS@CQDs4 was further mixed with 1 ml solution of different metal cations (Li+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Gd3+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Hg2+ K+, Na+, Fe2+) for testing the selectivity [26]. The developed film was also placed in the respective solution to visualize the chromogenic changes in the film under UV light exposure.

Experiments were performed in triplicate, and results are expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD). Statistical analysis was conducted using one-way ANOVA, and significant differences (p < 0.05) between means were determined with Duncan's multiple range test at a 95 % confidence interval using SPSS software (IBM, SPSS, Statistics, 25).

The crystalline structure of Taro Starch and CQDs obtained from residual slurry left after starch extraction from Colocasia esculenta was assessed by XRD analysis (Fig. 2a and b). The characteristic peaks for CQDs were appeared at 2θ = 23.21°, 29.6°, 31.6°, 36.1°, 39.66°, 43.4°, and 47.4° corresponding to (002), (102), (331), (105), (111), (200), (110) plane in CQDs (Fig. 2b). The crystalline size of CQDs was found to be 35.4 nm. The peaks were found to be consistent with existing literature [27,28]. The extracted taro starch displayed the prominent doublet peak at 2θ = 17.1° and 17.9° corresponding to (226) and (235) plane (Fig. 2a). These observed peaks were associated with the A-type allomorph in extracted starch from taro [29]. The other peaks were observed at 15.14°, 23.3°, 26.6° and 30.9° associated with (204), (221), (117) and (103) plane in taro starch. The resulting crystalline size was 17.8 nm with respect to 2θ = 17.9. Fig. 2b displayed the XRD patterns for blended films based on control (TS) and its bi-composite with CQDs i.e. Taro starch-CQDs 0.4 % (TS@CQDs1), Taro starch-CQDs 0.8 % (TS@CQDs2), Taro starch-CQDs 2 % (TS@CQDs3), and Taro starch-CQDs 4 % (TS@CQDs4) respectively. The control film of TS showed a peak at 19.7°. The spectra indicated the amorphous nature of the TS@CQDs film (Fig. 2c). This amorphous character is mainly explained due to the retrograding of starch during the casting process, which further enhanced the chain mobility and reducing the crystalline structure [15]. This conversion of crystalline into amorphous structure is highly useful to generate soft and flexible structure in films. Additionally, the presence of CQDs has not produced any alternations in the peak position of pure TS film. However, the composite films of TS with CQDs showed the existence of minor shoulder peaks at 15.2° and 30.2°, verifying the good compatibility of CQDs with TS (Fig. 2c).

Fig. 2ai-ci showed the FTIR spectra of Taro Starch, CQDs and composite films of TS containing (0.4, 0.8, 2 and 4 wt% of CQDs). The existence of broad absorption band at 3315 cm−1 and 2919 cm−1 in the case of CQDs (Fig. 2bi) was mainly aroused due to the presence –OH and -CH functional group over the exterior surface of CQDs [30]. The other absorption bands at 2342 cm−1 and 1650 cm−1 were attributed to the vibrations of -C-H and CC stretching [31]. The peaks at 1013 cm−1 and 1364 cm−1 were attributed to the C-O-C and CH stretching vibrations in CQDs derived from residual slurry left after the extraction of starch.

The broad absorption peak at 3276 cm−1 corresponded to -OH stretching vibration in extracted taro starch (Fig. 2ai and ci) and its corresponding film [15]. However, the presence of CQDs in composite films enhanced the peak intensity. This enhancement in intensity was mainly explained due to the even dispersion of CQDs in the composite film (Fig. 2ci). The CH stretching of methyl group was observed at 2929 cm−1 in pure taro starch and its composite films [30]. The peak at 1657 cm−1 was mainly aroused due to the presence of carbonyl and amide group of taro starch and CQDs in the composite films. The band at 1150 and 1012 cm−1 corresponded to CO stretching vibration and associated with the polymer blend structure in composite films. The pyranose ring in the glucose monomer was also vibrating at 1150 cm−1 in taro starch [32].

The morphological characteristic of extracted taro starch was assessed by SEM analysis. The taro starch displayed the existence of a polyhedral shape with apparent granules with an average size of 1 μm (Fig. 3ai to aiii) [11,13]. The obtained CQDs have displayed nearly spherical morphology in SEM and TEM images (Fig. 3bi to biii). However, the obtained TS film was found to be quite smooth without any cracks [15]. The observed contrast variations in specific regions of the TS film could be attributed to the presence of partially gelatinized starch molecules. The smooth surface of TS films was explained due to the existence of hydrogen bonding among thermoplastic starch under high temperature and pressure [33]. The relatively uniform and smooth films were observed with TS@CQDs1 and TS@CQDs2, signifying the improved compatibility between the TS and CQDs. However, the existence of gelatinized molecules of starch was also visible in TS@CQDs1 and TS@CQDs2.

With further increasing the loading amount of CQDs in TS has displayed the noticeable variations in the surface morphology of TS films. As the added amount of CQDs increased from 0.8 % to 2 %, the surface of the film became wavier in nature with continuous and uniform structures. The interaction between CQDs and TS is responsible for the observed morphological changes. The compact structure in formed films were also be affected by the existence of hydrogen bonding between the surface functional groups of CQDs in the TS matrix. The TS film containing 4 % CQDs showed rough surface morphology. This mainly explained due to the enhanced compactness in the film due to the presence of excess amount of CQDs. The existence of whiteness in the film was mainly explained due to the presence of starch granules and increased concentration of CQDs in the film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal stability of TS and TS@CQDs films
ce:section.ce:para: The thermal properties of TS films along with their bio-composites with CQDs were analyzed by estimating the variations in their mass under controlled heating process using TGA (Fig. 4a). The TGA thermograms of different films displayed a two-stage degradation process with respective mass loss of >26 % in the temperature range of 40–210 °C. The initial decline in the mass % >100 °C was related to the moisture loss from the films [15]. However, this mass percentage loss in TS@CQDs4 was around 15 % i.e. comparatively less than the other prepared films. The lesser degradation was mainly explained due to the presence of less adsorbed moisture over the surface of TS@CQDs4 based films. This behavioural aspect was explained due to the more hydrophobic nature of TS@CQDs based films in comparison to other films.

The second mass % loss was detected between 210 and 360 °C. This weight loss was mainly explained due to the thermal disintegration of the starch matrix in the prepared films [34–36]. Additionally, the functional groups over the surface of CQDs were also disintegrated in this temperature range. Moreover, the lower concentrations of CQDs in the TS matrix had not displayed any kind of significant variations in the thermal transition behaviour of TS film. In comparison, TS@CQDs4 film displayed more resistant to thermal degradation in this temperature range. The probable cause for this outcome may be explained with the higher stability of formed films due to more hydrogen bonding among the surface functional groups of CQDs and starch molecules. The carbonization of prepared films was occurred above 450 °C, with final variation in the residual amount varied between 5 and 7 %. To summarize, the outcome supports the higher stability of prepared films up to 40 °C for pure TS. This stability was further enhanced with the addition of CQDs. TS@CQDs4 based films were found to be quite stable up to 130 °C, which is higher than the pasteurization temperature of <100 °C or the steam sterilization process carried out in the industrial sector to remove pathogenic bacterial infection during food packaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical performance of TS and TS@CQDs films
ce:section.ce:para: The associated mechanical properties of TS films were also assessed to check their future prospects in packaging applications. The comparative effect of adding different amount of CQDs in TS films on their tensile strength, elongation at break and stress vs. strain plot, percentage values were illustrated in Fig. 4b-c and Fig. S1 [37–40]. The incorporation of CQDs has displayed encouraging transformations in the tensile strength and young's modules values of composite films in comparison to pure TS film. The respective value of Young's modulus value was highest for TS@CQDs4. The addition of 4 % CQDs into the TS films led to the highest tensile strength value of ≈4.635 MPa which was around 92 % superior to the pure taro starch film (Fig. 4b). The elongation at break percentage has also displayed a linear increment in the values in comparison to pure TS film (Fig. 4d). The outcomes were mainly attributed to the enhanced interactions between starch and CQDs, due to the existence of higher degree of hydrogen and covalent bonding between the surface functional groups of CQDs and starch matrix.

The associated mechanical properties of TS films were also assessed to check their future prospects in packaging applications. The comparative effect of adding different amount of CQDs in TS films on their tensile strength, elongation at break and stress vs. strain plot, percentage values were illustrated in Fig. 4b-c and Fig. S1 [37–40]. The incorporation of CQDs has displayed encouraging transformations in the tensile strength and young's modules values of composite films in comparison to pure TS film. The respective value of Young's modulus value was highest for TS@CQDs4. The addition of 4 % CQDs into the TS films led to the highest tensile strength value of ≈4.635 MPa which was around 92 % superior to the pure taro starch film (Fig. 4b). The elongation at break percentage has also displayed a linear increment in the values in comparison to pure TS film (Fig. 4d). The outcomes were mainly attributed to the enhanced interactions between starch and CQDs, due to the existence of higher degree of hydrogen and covalent bonding between the surface functional groups of CQDs and starch matrix.

The results were comparable with the reported data [41–43]. The incorporation of CQDs led to an increase in tensile strength to a greater extent [42]. Thus, the modification of TS films with CQDs was favourable to improve the strength of the TS film. The stronger interactions and the high dispersity of CQDs-based nanofillers have synergistically improves the mechanical aptitude of TS film. In a particular investigation, the mechanical strength of carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) films was notably enhanced with the incorporation of 5 wt% of CQDs. This enhancement is attributed to the uniform distribution of CQDs within the polymer matrix due to their size and hydrophilic nature [44]. Similarly, Chen et al., 2019 observed the enhancement in the tensile strength of the starch matrix via the addition of GQDs in the reaction medium. Furthermore, the enhancement displayed a linear increment with the concentration of GQDs ranging from 1.4 wt% to 10.9 wt% in the starch matrix. The edges of adjacent one-dimensional GQDs sheets have the tendency to join and lead to efficient load transfer between the starch matrix and the GQDs filler [45]. Riahi et al., 2024 prepared bioplastic film using cellulose nanofibres/pullulan impregnated with zinc-doped carbon dots derived from avocado peel [46]. The composite films have possessed better tensile strength and elongation at break values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of moisture content with water vapour permeability rate
ce:section.ce:para: The moisture content, water solubility and water vapour transmission rate of TS and TS@CQDs films were compared to assess the application of developed films in food wrapping purposes. As illustrated in Fig. 5 , that all the prepared films hold some moisture content in them. This is mainly explained due to the presence of glycerol moieties in the taro starch films which further possess the potential of surface moisture absorption [47]. However, the moisture content value displayed significant variations among TS film and TS @CQDs film. For instance, the moisture content value of TS@CQDs1 film was decreased by 34.6 % due to the assimilation of a small content of CQDs as external nanofillers. However, this decrease was observed to be 57.3 % for TS@CQDs4 film. This reduction in moisture content value was explained due to the enhanced interaction between the polymer and the functional filler, which further reduced the water absorption capacity in the films. Besides the hydrophilic nature of used nanofillers, the crytallinity possessed by CQDs is mainly responsible for the reduction in moisture content. The obtained results were well comparable with the literature [48–50]. The film by using N-doped carbon dots and chitosan/polyvinylpyrrolidone, have possessed reduced moisture content and water solubility due to the presence of CQDs [48]. Thus, our result supports the potential usage of prepared films in packaging where minimal moisture is highly recommended for the safety of packaged food items. The modification in the mechanical strength via the addition of CQDs in TS films further supported the reduction in diffusion of water molecules in the prepared films.

The water vapour transmission rate (WVTR) measures the quantity of water vapour that permeates through the analyzed film. The rate of water vapour passage is influenced by the film's pore size and hydrophilicity [51]. Moreover, the highly dispersed CQDs in TS matrix lengthened the pathway for water vapour. The addition of CQDs to TS films has also reduced the water transmission rate to 67 % as compared to pure TS films. The results were comparable with the finding from Shanmathy et al., (2021) where the addition of bentonite starch based films has reduced WVTR value [52]. This decrease due to the addition of filler may be attributed to the convoluted path created by the uniformly dispersed filler in the prepared film. Similarly, the incorporation of CQDs in Carrageenan/alginate-based functional films has reduced the values of water vapour permeability which is consistent with our result. [53].

The results showed the interaction among the CQDs with TS matrix, which further substantiates the mechanical and surface morphological properties of developed films. Water solubility values of the prepared films are also considered as an important parameter to assess the water resistance abilities of the developed films. Thus, all the prepared films using CQDs nanocomposites with TS were checked for their solubility analysis after immersion in aqueous medium. As per the data presented in Fig. 5, the film developed from taro starch showed a solubility of 50 ± 2.1 %, associated with the fractional dissolution of starch and glycerol molecules. The TS@CQDs1 film with 0.4 % of CQDs displayed slight decrement in the solubility value to 47 ± 3 %. The solubility value was further reduced to 44 ± 1.4 %, 38 ± 1.8 % and 30 ± 1.2 % for TS@CQDs2, TS@CQDs3 and TS@CQDs4 films respectively, indicating the stability and increment in the hydrophobic character in the films. Similar result was observed by Yang et al., (2024) where the addition of 5 % CQDs, has exhibited a decrement in both water solubility and water vapour transmission rate [54].

The hydrophobicity of developed surface of TS and bio-composite films integrated with different percentages of CQDs were evaluated by measuring the water contact angle (WCA) from the surface of films (Fig. 6 ). The WCA of TS film was enhanced by increasing the doping concentration of CQDs from 0.4 % to 4 %. The WCA value of pure TS film was found to be 40.6°. However, the WCA value was 52.2°, 69.5°, 73.5° and 89.6° for TS@CQDs1, TS@CQDs2, TS@CQDs3 and TS@CQDs4 films respectively. The variation in the WCA values was associated with the surface compactness of the prepared films. The added CQDs as nanofiller influenced the polymer matrix and thereby varied the WCA values of the prepared films. These outcomes are in support with the data obtained for moisture content and underlined the substantial significance of CQDs in improving the water resistance properties in developed films.

The obtained data offered strong confirmation that the addition of CQDs in TS blended films holds efficient moisture barrier ability as compared to TS films. The reduction in the water solubility values with the incorporation of CQDs in polymer matrix further strengthened the polymer network contact and it's cross-linking. The results were comparable with the study conducted by Althawab, et al., (2024) [55]. The increment in the concentration of CDs concentration in TPC hybrid films has displayed a linear increment in the WCA values [55]. These carbon-based materials at the nanoscale level add surface roughness to the TPC film, resulting in the formation of microstructures that capture air pockets and decrease the amount of contact between the solid and liquid [55]. Guo et al., (2024), found that the pure gelatin film was more hydrophilic in comparison to CQDs doped films [56]. The water contact angle (WCA) of the carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) film was significantly enhanced with the incorporation of carbon quantum dots (CQD) [44]. The formation of hydrogen bonds between the polymer and the CQDs likely reduces the number of hydroxyl groups in the CMC structure, thereby decreasing surface hydrophilicity and increasing the WCA. The WCA also increases by the reinforcement of bentonite as filler in taro starch based bioplastic films [52]. Thus, the developed films hold more water resistance which is primarily required for its usage in commercial applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant activities of TS and TS@CQDs films
ce:section.ce:para: The analysis of the antioxidant activity of the prepared bio-composite films is one of the important properties of the developed bioplastic films in food packaging applications. The existence of antioxidative properties in the films has a significant impact on enhancing the shelf life of the packed food materials. Thus, the antioxidant properties of the developed TS films by the addition of CQDs were estimated by DPPH assays (Fig. 7 ). The free radical scavenging efficacy of pure TS film was found to be 1.2 % against DPPH. The significant boost in the antioxidative properties was observed with the addition of CQDs in TS films. The percentage inhibition values were 13.64, 14.72, 20.15 and 51.9 % for TS@CQDs1, TS@CQDs2, TS@CQDs3 and TS@CQDs4, respectively. The outcomes confirmed the role of CQDs as potential functional material in TS films and enhanced its scope as packaging material. These results were consistent with the literature values where the incorporation of CQDs has displayed positive impact on the antioxidant properties of the developed films. [30,38]. However, the future prospective of the developed materials has also been modified by increasing the anti-oxidative potential beyond 52 % in near future by developing new fillers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biodegradation studies of TS and TS@CQDs films
ce:section.ce:para: The garden soil was used for the soil degradation test in flower pot. The different biofilms were buried 15 cm down the pot. The extent of biodegradation was checked after 6 days for all the biofilms. The biobased nature of the prepared film supports the effective degradation of soil under natural conditions. The existing components including starch extracted from taro waste, glycerol is mainly helpful for the microbial growth in the sample with further supports the degradation of the prepared films. From Fig. 8 , it was detected that the degradation efficiency of the film was reduced with an increase in the concentration of CQDs in the film. On comparing the data, it was further observed that pure TS film showed a higher decrement of weight loss in the 6 days as compared to TS@CQDs4 film. The lower rate of degradation in TS@CQDs4 film can be explained due to the higher rate of intermolecular interactions among TS and CQDs films. However, it was observed that the percentage weight loss in all the buried films was increased with an increment in the number of days of soil burial. All the buried films were completely degraded after 30 days with no sign of film debris in the soil. The higher weight loss of TS films as compared to bio-composite film was explained due to its hydrophilic nature which further holds a higher percentage of moisture from the surroundings and degraded faster.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing ability of TS and TS@CQDs films
ce:section.ce:para: The application of the developed films was further assessed as metal ion sensing template [22]. Initially, the casting solutions of each film were diluted and stirred continuously for 24 h. After stirring, the respective solution was sonicated for 15 min. The fluorescence measurements were performed as a function of excitation wavelength for each solution (Fig. 9 ). On comparing the results, it was observed that the emission intensity was dependent on the concentration of added CQDs in the casting sample. Thus, TS@CQDs4 gives the maximum intensity as compared to other solution. Therefore, the metal ion sensing experiments were done using TS@CQDs4 as standard solution. The respective diluted casting solution of TS@CQDs4 was further mixed with 1 mL solution of different metal cations (Li+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Gd3+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Hg2+ K+, Na+, Fe2+,) for testing the selectivity. Out of all chosen metal cations, TS@CQDs4 has shown selectivity towards Fe2+ ions (Fig. 10a). Furthermore, for the sensitivity studies, fluorometric titration was carried out with different concentrations of Fe2+ ranging from 15 to 50 μM in reaction media (Fig. 10b). On interpreting the data, it was observed that the fluorescence intensity displayed linear increment with the increase in the concentration of the Fe2+.

The quantification limit was assessed by plotting the calibration curve of fluorescence intensity as a function of different concentrations of Fe2+. The Standard 3σ IUPAC criteria were further used to calculate the detection limit value (Fig. 10c). The interference study of the developed fluorescent sensor was carried out by taking 1 ml of diluted casting solution of TS@CQDs4 in the presence of 0.5 ml of Fe2+ and 0.5 ml of other metal cations (Fig. 10d). The samples were properly sonicated prior to get the emission spectra. On analyzing the data, it was found that the presence of various metal ions had no effect on the emission intensity of TS@CQDs4. However, in the presence of other interfering ions, the intensity pattern and peak position of the Fe2+ ion was found to be unaffected. Hence, the prepared system was extremely efficient and selective for the Fe2+ ion.

Furthermore, the quenching of fluorescence spectra of TS@CQDs4 solution was represented by the Stern Volmer plot as shown in Fig. 9b [57]. The ratio of Fo/Fn was measured in which Fo and Fn represent PL emission of TS@CQDs4 solution in the absence and presence of Fe2+ ions respectively. The fluorescence results showed that increasing concentration of Fe2+ ions can led to increase in Fo/Fn value and the color intensity of CQDs was significantly affected (Fig. 10). The primary indices for evaluating the practical application of a sensor are the linear range, limit of detection (LOD), and limit of quantification (LOQ) [57]. Fortunately, linear correlation was found between the Fe2+ ions concentration and intensity ratio starting from 15 to 50 μm with R2 > 0.98 (Fig. 9c). The obtained graphs were found consistent with the Stern Volmer equation.
(5)
Fₒ Fₙ
= Ksv
Q
+ 1
where Q represents the concentration of the quencher and Ksv is the Stern Volmer quenching constant. Optimising the integrated area under the peak, the Limit of detection and the Limit of quantification were also determined using the following formulas [58].
(6)
LOD =
3 × S . D
Slope
(7)

10 × S . D
Slope
where S·D represents the standard deviation. Under the optimal conditions, the obtained values for LOD and LOQ were found to be 0.97 μM and 3.24 μM respectively in the fluorescence-based sensing.

To develop the portable sensing template, the prepared TS@CQDs4 films were further used to test the presence of Fe2+ ions (Fig. 11 ). The developed film was placed in the respective solution of metal to visualize the chromogenic changes in the film under UV light exposure (Fig. 9). Out of all chosen metal ions, it was observed that the TS@CQDs4 film was colorless in presence of Fe2+ ions in UV light exposure. However, color remained intact in presence of other metal ions. Thus, the prepared film was extremely efficient and selective for the Fe2+ ion and provides an effective sensing template for Fe2+ ions.

Looking back at the available literature data, it was observed that polyethylene (PE) films were mainly used for sensing applications. However, the non-biodegradable nature of PE films has possessed a harmful and deteriorating impact on the environment. Thus, the current study proposed a solution for the replacement of conventional plastic with the biodegradable and biocompatible films for in sensing applications and holds significant potential in food industry for quality check purposes.

The current work highlights the fabrication of TS and TS@CQDs films with the composition of CQDs varied from 0.4 % to 4 %. FT-IR, XRD and TGA analysis confirmed the successful incorporation of CQDs in TS films. The integration of CQDs has significantly enhanced the fluorescence, mechanical and the hydrophobic characteristics of the bio-composite films in comparison to pure TS. The incorporation of 4 % CQDs in bio-composite films has increased the tensile strength to 4.635 MPa which is 96 % higher as compared to pure TS films. The WCA was further enhanced from 40.6° to 89.6° for TS@CQDs4 in comparison to TS films. On the basis of the fluorescence properties of prepared film via the integration of CQDs in TS matrix has further been employed to develop the chromogenic sensor for iron in aqueous medium. The sensitivity of the developed sensor showed concentration dependency of Fe2+ ion in aqueous medium. The presence of hydroxide and carbonyl groups in taro starch based film provides superior biodegradable qualities in prepared bio-plastic. Our outcomes of this work hold significant relevance to provide a smart sensory and biodegradable probe for metal ion sensing by using waste resources, thus offering a better and sustainable alternative to environmental remediation applications.
